_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Hancock County, IN. Record survival and courthouse history for the National Road county centered on Greenfield. Well-preserved records from 1828 organization.

previous_courthouses.fate: Brick courthouse. Served through the Civil War. Replaced by current Romanesque Revival building.

record_losses.description: Hancock County's records are well-preserved from the 1828 organization. No major fire or flood damage. Records run continuously.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Hancock County, the records survive. The county's agricultural character means most families appear in deed records and agricultural census schedules.
record_losses.source: Indiana Commission on Public Records. Guide to Indiana County Records.

deeds.repository: Hancock County Recorder; Indiana State Archives

wills_estates.repository: Hancock County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

marriages.repository: Hancock County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

court_minutes.repository: Hancock County Clerk; Indiana State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1830-1940. Agricultural schedules particularly valuable for farming county.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Agricultural schedules document farm size, value, livestock, and crops.

substitute_sources.description: Methodist and Baptist churches served most of Hancock County's population. Records supplement civil records before vital registration.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Indiana United Methodist Archives at DePauw University
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Baptisms, marriages, and burials. Church transfer records track migration.
substitute_sources.source: Indiana United Methodist Archives, DePauw University.

narrative_hooks: Hancock County's 1897 courthouse still stands at Greenfield — {ancestor_name}'s records may be in the same building where the original documents were filed.
Before 1882, a missing birth record for {ancestor_name} in Hancock County is standard — check church records and the federal census.
Hancock County was a farming county, and farmers appear in the records — deed books, agricultural census schedules, and tax lists all track {ancestor_name}'s land and livelihood.
If {ancestor_name} passed through Hancock County on the National Road, the county records may not have captured {pronoun_object} at all — transient families left fewer traces than settled ones.
source_references: Indiana Commission on Public Records. Guide to Indiana County Records.
Binford, John H. History of Hancock County. Greenfield: King and Binford, 1882.
Indiana United Methodist Archives finding aids, DePauw University.
